passages.explanation: Some people tell Jesus about Galileans whose blood Pilate mixed with their sacrifices — killed while worshipping. Jesus asks: do you think these Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans because they suffered this way? I tell you, no! But unless you repent, you too will all perish. Or those eighteen who died when the tower in Siloam fell on them — do you think they were more guilty than all the others living in Jerusalem? I tell you, no! But unless you repent, you too will all perish. Jesus refuses the assumption that tragedy indicates special guilt. He redirects: the real danger isn't random catastrophe — it's unrepentance. Everyone is equally vulnerable without God.

Jesus tells a parable: a man has a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He goes to look for fruit on it but finds none. He tells the vineyard keeper: for three years I've been coming to look for fruit on this fig tree and haven't found any. Cut it down — why should it use up the soil? The keeper replies: sir, leave it alone for one more year. I'll dig around it and fertilise it. If it bears fruit next year, fine. If not, then cut it down. The parable is about patience with a deadline. God is patient, but patience isn't the same as permanence. The fig tree has a season to respond.

On a Sabbath Jesus is teaching in one of the synagogues. A woman is there who has been crippled by a spirit for eighteen years — she's bent over and can't straighten up at all. When Jesus sees her, he calls her forward: woman, you are set free from your infirmity. He puts his hands on her and immediately she straightens up and praises God. The synagogue leader is indignant because Jesus healed on the Sabbath. He tells the crowd: there are six days for work — come and be healed on those days, not on the Sabbath. Jesus responds: you hypocrites! Doesn't each of you untie your ox or donkey on the Sabbath and lead it to water? Shouldn't this woman, a daughter of Abraham whom Satan has kept bound for eighteen long years, be set free on the Sabbath day? His opponents are humiliated, but the people are delighted. Jesus calls her 'a daughter of Abraham' — a title of dignity and belonging. Eighteen years of bondage ended on the day supposedly reserved for rest.

Jesus asks: what is the kingdom of God like? What shall I compare it to? It's like a mustard seed, which a man takes and plants in his garden. It grows and becomes a tree, and the birds perch in its branches. Again: what shall I compare the kingdom of God to? It's like yeast that a woman mixes into about sixty pounds of flour until it works all through the dough. Two images of the kingdom: something tiny that becomes huge, and something hidden that transforms everything it touches. The kingdom doesn't start with a political revolution — it starts small and spreads organically.

Jesus goes through towns and villages, teaching as he makes his way to Jerusalem. Someone asks: Lord, are only a few people going to be saved? Jesus replies: make every effort to enter through the narrow door, because many will try to enter and won't be able to. Once the owner of the house gets up and closes the door, you'll stand outside knocking and pleading: sir, open the door for us. He'll answer: I don't know you or where you come from. Then you'll say: we ate and drank with you, and you taught in our streets. He'll reply: I don't know you — away from me, all you evildoers! There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God but you yourselves thrown out. People will come from east and west, north and south, and will take their places at the feast in the kingdom of God. Indeed, there are those who are last who will be first, and first who will be last. Proximity to Jesus isn't the same as relationship with Jesus. Having heard him teach in your streets doesn't mean you entered through the door.

Some Pharisees come and warn Jesus: leave this place — Herod wants to kill you. Jesus replies: go tell that fox — I will keep driving out demons and healing people today and tomorrow, and on the third day I will reach my goal. In any case, I must press on today and tomorrow and the next day — surely no prophet can die outside Jerusalem! Then he laments: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you — how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing. Look, your house is left to you desolate. I tell you, you will not see me again until you say: blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord. Jesus calls Herod a fox — dismissive, not afraid. His timeline isn't set by political threats but by divine purpose. And his grief over Jerusalem is genuine — he wants to protect the city that will kill him.
